request.question: United 300 and 300 were both made in what country?

title: The Biggest Loser
Foxburg Country Club
University of Nebraska Cornhusker Marching Band
Transportation in Canada
Human trafficking in Uganda
United 300
300 (film)
North Country Community College
Health in Morocco
Maynard carbine
sentences: The Biggest Loser is a reality television show which started in the United States in 2004.
The show centers on overweight and obese contestants attempting to lose the most weight and to fight for a cash prize.
There are different variations of "The Biggest Loser" around the world.
Each country has made its own adaptation to the show; however, the contestants always have the same goal: to lose the highest percentage of weight (or most weight) to become the Biggest Loser.
There is no minimum or maximum weight limit for the show but most males tend to weigh over or near 300 lb (136 kg).
Females tend to weigh over or near 200 lb (91 kg).
Foxburg Country Club, established in 1887, is the oldest golf course in continuous use in the United States.
It is located in Foxburg, Clarion County, Pennsylvania, United States of America, approximately 55 mi north of Pittsburgh on a hill rising about 300 feet above the Allegheny River.
The course was listed in 2007 as Foxburg Country Club and Golf Course on the National Register of Historic Places.
The clubhouse contains the American Golf Hall of Fame.
The University of Nebraska Cornhusker Marching Band (also known as the Marching Red or The Pride of All Nebraska) is the marching band of the University of Nebraska.
It performs at all home football games.
Founded in 1879, the Marching Red is one of the oldest and best-known collegiate marching bands in the United States.
The band consists of 300 students from over 60 different academic majors from across the campus.
Thanks to the success of the Husker football program, the Marching Red is also one of the most traveled bands in the country having participated in all of the major bowl games.

They have appeared multiple times at the Rose, Fiesta, Orange, Sugar, Cotton, Sun, and Alamo Bowls, and have made single appearances at the Bluebonnet, Liberty, Holiday, Citrus, Independence and Gator Bowls.
On November 19, 2011, the Cornhusker Marching Band performed at Michigan Stadium in front of the largest audience ever to watch the band.

Transportation in Canada, the world's second-largest country in total area, is dedicated to having an efficient, high-capacity multimodal transport spanning often vast distances between natural resource extraction sites, agricultural and urban areas.

Canada's transportation system includes more than 1400000 km of roads, 10 major international airports, 300 smaller airports, 72093 km of functioning railway track, and more than 300 commercial ports and harbours that provide access to the Pacific, Atlantic and Arctic oceans as well as the Great Lakes and the St. Lawrence Seaway.
In 2005, the transportation sector made up 4.2% of Canada's GDP, compared to 3.7% for Canada's mining and oil and gas extraction industries.
Uganda is a source and destination country for men, women, and children trafficked for the purposes of forced labor and sexual exploitation.
Ugandan children are trafficked within the country, as well as to Canada, Egypt, the United Arab Emirates, and Saudi Arabia for forced labor and commercial sexual exploitation.
Karamojong women and children are sold in cattle markets or by intermediaries and forced into situations of domestic servitude, sexual exploitation, herding, and begging.

Security companies in Kampala recruit Ugandans to serve as security guards in Iraq where, at times, their travel documents and pay have reportedly been withheld as a means to prevent their departure; these cases may constitute trafficking.
Pakistani, Indian, and Chinese workers are reportedly trafficked to Uganda, and Indian networks traffic Indian children to the country for sexual exploitation.
Children from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (D.R.C.), Rwanda, and Burundi are trafficked to Uganda for agricultural labor and commercial sexual exploitation.

Until August 2006, the terrorist rebel organization, Lord’s Resistance Army (LRA), abducted children and adults in northern Uganda to serve as soldiers, sex slaves, and porters; while no further abductions of Ugandan children have been reported, at least 300 additional people, mostly children, were abducted during the reporting period in the Central African Republic and the D.R.C.
The Government of Uganda does not fully comply with the minimum standards for the elimination of trafficking; however, it is making significant efforts to do so.
United 300 is an American short film that parodies "United 93" (2006) and "300" (2007).
It won the MTV Movie Spoof Award at the MTV Movie Awards 2007.
The short was created by Andy Signore along with some of his friends.
During his speech, he said, roughly, "The film was not making fun of a tragedy; it was a tribute to those who stood up against tyranny".
300 is a 2006 American epic war film based on the 1998 comic series "300" by Frank Miller and Lynn Varley.
Both are fictionalized retellings of the Battle of Thermopylae within the Persian Wars.
The film was directed by Zack Snyder, while Miller served as executive producer and consultant.
It was filmed mostly with a super-imposition chroma key technique, to help replicate the imagery of the original comic book.
North Country Community College is a two-year college of the State University of New York located in Upstate New York.
Founded in 1967, the main campus is in Saranac Lake, New York, and serves Franklin and Essex Counties; enrollment is approximately 1500 students.
It employs 143 permanent and 300 adjunct faculty.
North Country is the only public college located within the Adirondack Park.
The College, located in New York's North Country Region, has additional locations in Malone and Ticonderoga.
In August 2010, North Country Community College was ranked Best Community College in New York and 22nd among the Best Community Colleges in the United States by "Washington Monthly".

The college has also been ranked one of the Top 50 fastest growing community colleges in the United States in "Community College Week Magazine", 101 Best for Vets Colleges in the United States by "Military Times" and a Top 15% Military Friendly Colleges in the U.S., by gijobs.com.
Morocco became an independent country in 1956.
At that time there were only 400 private practitioners and 300 public health physicians in the entire country.
By 1992, the government had thoroughly improved their health care service and quality.
Health care was made available to over 70% of the population.
Programs and courses to teach health and hygiene have been introduced to inform parents and children on how to correctly care for their own and their families' health.
The Maynard carbine was a breech-loaded carbine used by cavalry in the American Civil War.
The First Model was manufactured between 1858 and 1859.
About 5,000 were made.
In United States service it was distributed to the 9th Pennsylvania and 1st Wisconsin cavalry regiments, United States Marines aboard the USS "Saratoga" and the United States Revenue Cutter Service.
About 3,000 Maynard carbines were in Confederate hands during the war; 5,000 in .35 caliber were purchased by Florida, 650 in .50 by Georgia, and 325 in .50 and 300 in .35 by Mississippi.
Around 800 were purchased by militias in South Carolina and Louisiana.
The Second Model or Model 1863 was manufactured between 1863 and 1865.
Over 20,000 were made.
This model lacked the tape primer and stock patchbox, and was used by the 9th and 11th Indiana cavalry regiments and 11th Regiment Tennessee Volunteer Cavalry, among others.
The Maynard had a good reputation for long-range accuracy, and Confederate sharpshooters made extensive use of it, especially during the Siege of Charleston.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: United 300 and 300 were both made in what country?
hop1_docs: Hansen High School | Hansen High School is a high school in Hansen, Idaho.  They were the 2003 state track division 1A champions.They went to 2009 state track in the 300 hurdles, and 800m.  Their rivals are the Murtaugh Red Devils.  Hansen high school has about 300+ students.  It offers athletic programs for both girls and boys such as football, volleyball, basketball, cross country, and track.

Fortschritt ZT 300 | ZT 300 is a series of 20-kN-tractors produced from 1967 to 1984 by the VEB Traktorenwerk Schönebeck.  It succeeded the "RS14 Famulus" series and unlike the Famulus, the ZT 300 series was sold under the brand name Fortschritt.  "ZT 300" stands for the ZT 300 series tractors as well as the initial tractor Fortschritt ZT 300.  In total, 72.382 tractors were made.  The most built model was the ZT 303 introduced in 1972 with automatic all-wheel-drive; in the early 1980s it would cost 81.000 Mark.  Starting in 1983, the ZT 300 series was succeeded by the ZT 320.

Farman F.300 | The Farman F.300 and F.310 were airliners built in France in the early 1930s.  Built to the same general trimotor layout popular with manufacturers of the time, they were high-wing strut braced monoplanes with fixed tailskid undercarriage.  The cockpit and passenger compartment were fully enclosed.  Most went to equip Farman's own airline, whose twelve F.300 variants made up half its fleet in 1931.

KTM 300 | The KTM 300 is a series of 2-stroke enduro off-road motorcycles made by KTM.  They are the Penton-inspired 1984 300 GS, the 300 DMX, the dirt only 300 MXC, European road legal 300 EXC and their current US counterparts the 300 XC (close-ratio) and 300 XC-W (wide-ratio gear box) respectively.  The KTM 300 is designed for difficult off-road conditions.  The 300 EXC and the 300 XC-W have a small headlight, speedometer, tail-light and somewhat softer linkless PDS suspension.  Starting in 2009 all 300's came with electric start.

Northern 300 | The Northern 300 was a NASCAR Winston Cup Series stock car race held in 1958, 1959, and from 1967 to 1972 at Trenton Speedway in Trenton, New Jersey.  The debut race in 1958 was 500 laps and 500 mi in length, which was cut down to 150 laps, 150 mi for 1959.  The 1967 and 1968 races were both 300 laps, covering 300 mi .  The track was lengthened from 1 mile to 1.5 miles for 1969, and the final four races were cut down from 300 to 200 laps, while still covering a 300-mile distance.  A race in Trenton was on the 1973 schedule but was rained out on race day and ultimately cancelled.  A 1974 race was on the Winston Cup schedule before the season, but was replaced by the Purolator 500 at Pocono Raceway.  Richard Petty was the only driver to win more than once at Trenton, winning three times.

Mervyn Cawston | Mervyn Cawston (born 4 February 1952) is an English former professional footballer who played as a goalkeeper.  Active in both England and the United States, Cawston made over 300 career appearances.

Morane-Saulnier MS.300 | The Morane-Saulnier MS.300 and MS.301 were French parasol wing introductory trainer aircraft, first flown in 1930.  They differed only in engine type.  Neither reached production but were developed into two similar trainers, the MS.230 and MS.315, which were made in large numbers.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - United 300: NOT mentioned in the provided passages.
  - 300: multiple passages contain "300" in titles:
    - Fortschritt ZT 300 — "produced from 1967 to 1984 by the VEB Traktorenwerk Schönebeck." (Fortschritt ZT 300)
    - Farman F.300 — "airliners built in France in the early 1930s." (Farman F.300)
    - Morane-Saulnier MS.300 — "were French ... first flown in 1930." (Morane-Saulnier MS.300)
    - KTM 300 — "series ... made by KTM." (KTM 300)
    - Northern 300 — "a NASCAR ... at Trenton, New Jersey." (Northern 300)

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - "United 300" missing from passages — unknown entity/page.
  - "300" is ambiguous here: could refer to the film "300" (2006) or to any of the items above (aircraft, tractor, motorcycle, race). Passages do not include the film "300" page.
  - For some listed "300" items, the manufacturing country is explicit (Farman, Morane-Saulnier = France); for others it's not explicit in these passages (KTM 300, ZT 300 implicit GDR, etc.).

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Page or source establishing the country of origin for "United 300".
  - Clear identification of which "300" is intended (e.g., "300 (film)", "Farman F.300", "KTM 300", etc.) and the country where that specific "300" was made.

- retrieval_direction (prioritized):
  1. Retrieve the "United 300" page — search: "United 300 film Wikipedia" or "United 300 (short film) country of origin".
  2. Retrieve "300 (film)" — search: "300 (2006 film) Wikipedia" to get production country if the question refers to the film.
  3. Retrieve "300 (disambiguation)" or "300 (disambiguation) Wikipedia" to confirm which "300" is intended.
  4. If the intended "300" is a vehicle/aircraft, fetch specific pages: "KTM 300" (manufacturer country/KTM page) and "Fortschritt ZT 300" (confirm production country/GDR).
hop2_docs: Vought-Sikorsky VS-300 | The Vought-Sikorsky VS-300 (or S-46) was a single-engine helicopter designed by Igor Sikorsky.  It had a single three-blade rotor originally powered by a 75 horsepower (56 kW) engine.  The first "free" flight of the VS-300 was on 13 May 1940.  The VS-300 was the first successful single lifting rotor helicopter in the United States and the first successful helicopter to use a single vertical-plane tail rotor configuration for antitorque.  With floats attached, it became the first practical amphibious helicopter.

LAV-300 | The Cadillac Gage Textron LAV-300, originally named as the V-300, is a family of American light armored vehicles (LAVs) including up to 15 configurations.  It was originally created and designed by CG as a private venture project.

Schweizer 300 | The Sikorsky S-300 (formerly Hughes 300 and Schweizer 300) family of light utility helicopters was originally produced by Hughes Helicopters, as a development of the Hughes 269.  Later manufactured by Schweizer Aircraft, the basic design has been in production for almost 50 years.  The single, three-bladed main rotor and piston-powered S-300 is mostly used as a cost-effective platform for training and agriculture.

Maryland Route 300 | Maryland Route 300 (MD 300) is a state highway in the U.S. state of Maryland.  Known for most of its length as Sudlersville Road, the highway runs 13.55 mi from MD 213 in Church Hill east through Sudlersville to the Delaware state line, where the highway continues as Delaware Route 300 (DE 300).  Together with DE 300, DE 44, and DE 8, MD 300 forms an east–west connection between U.S. Route 301 (US 301) in northern Queen Anne's County and Dover, Delaware.  MD 300 between Church Hill and Dudley Corners was one of the original state roads marked for improvement in 1909.  The highway was completed between Church Hill and Sudlersville around 1920.  MD 300 between Sudlersville and the Delaware state line was constructed in the late 1920s and early 1930s.

300 Original Motion Picture Soundtrack | 300 is the soundtrack to the 2007 film "300".  It was composed by Tyler Bates and was released on March 6, 2007, three days before the film opened for public viewing.

300 Club | The 300 Club is the name given to those who have endured a range of temperature of 300° Fahrenheit (166°C) within a very short time.  The practice originated at Amundsen-Scott South Pole Station in Antarctica.

No. 300 Polish Bomber Squadron | No. 300 (Polish) "Land of Masovia" Bomber Squadron (Polish: "300 Dywizjon Bombowy "Ziemi Mazowieckiej"" ) was a Polish World War II bomber unit.  It was fighting alongside the Royal Air Force and operated from airfields in the United Kingdom.
response.summary_2: Insufficient information: "United 300" is not present in the provided passages and "300" is ambiguous. Some retrieved "300" items (Farman F.300 and Morane-Saulnier MS.300) were made in France, but there is no evidence showing that both "United 300" and the intended "300" were made in the same country.
